title: Service Permissions Schema
description: Defines a service's permission surface: actions, resources, permission IDs, roles, and default grants. Used by 01-register-service-permissions.sh to register the service with the Auth Mesh.

id.description: Unique service identifier. Used as org slug and permission prefix. Lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens.

version.description: Semver version of the permission schema

audience.description: Token audience identifier. Used in JWT aud claim for service-to-service auth.

maintainer.description: Contact email for the service owner

registration.description: Permission registration config sent to POST /permissions/registry/register

service.description: Service name for the registry. Usually matches service.id.

actions.description: Available actions (read, write, create, delete, execute, admin, etc.)

permissions.description: All permission IDs this service defines

intent.description: Why a user would need this permission

risk_level.description: Risk assessment for granting this permission

cost_impact.description: Whether this permission can trigger billable operations

default_grant.description: Whether this permission is granted to all new users by default

implies.description: Permission IDs this permission implies (admin permissions typically imply all others)

roles.description: Pre-defined role bundles mapping to permission sets

default.description: Whether this is the default role for new users. Exactly one role should be default.

tenant_field.description: Field name used for tenant scoping (usually org_id)

enforcement.description: Whether cross-tenant access is structurally blocked or permission-gated

end_users.description: Configuration for how end-users are provisioned

auto_provision.description: Whether users are auto-provisioned on org join

default_team_name.description: Name of the default team created for end-users (team-inherited model)

org_structure.description: Predefined org hierarchy pattern to materialize when a user registers via hosted login. The auth service's event handler reads this at runtime and creates the configured structure. Leaving this block out (or setting pattern 'flat') means no extra orgs are created — users simply join the end-users org with the default team, which is the standard behavior.

pattern.description:
Which org structure to build on registration. Available patterns:

• 'flat' — no extra orgs. Users join the end-users org and the default team only. This is the existing behavior and the safe default.

• 'workspace-per-user' — each new user gets a private nested org (a 'workspace') under the end-users org, with themselves as owner and their own default team. Useful for services where each user needs a personal space: their own API keys, their own teammates, their own settings. Similar to GitHub user namespaces, Notion personal workspaces, or Vercel personal scopes.

config.description: Pattern-specific configuration. Fields below apply to 'workspace-per-user'. For 'flat' this block is ignored — you can omit it entirely.

slug_template.description: Template for the workspace slug (the URL-friendly identifier). Variables: {email_prefix} — lowercased local part of the email (e.g. 'alice' from 'Alice@acme.com'), {short_id} — 6-char random hex suffix to prevent collisions between users with similar emails. Setup script 04 pre-substitutes {service_id} at config-write time so the runtime handler doesn't need to know your service id. Default: '{email_prefix}-{short_id}'.

name_template.description: Template for the workspace display name shown in UI / org listings. Variables: {email}, {email_prefix}. Default: "{email_prefix}'s workspace".

default_team_name.description: Display name for the team that holds default permissions inside each workspace. Default: 'Default'.

default_team_permissions.description: Permission IDs to grant to the workspace's default team. Usually left empty here — setup script 04 auto-populates this from your permissions[] array by selecting every permission where default_grant is true. Override only if the workspace team should carry a different permission set than your end-users team. Each ID must exist in your permissions[] array.

api_key.description: API key configuration for service-to-service auth

additional_permissions.description: Extra permissions needed on the API key beyond service permissions
